messages.content: For all of my prompts, Paraphrase (to get a 0% plagiarism score) without reducing the word count.
Certainly! Just go ahead and share the specific content you need paraphrased, and I'll help with that.

The Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)) regulations/bills in Nigeria originate from the introduction of the Labour Act of 1974 to the passage of the Labour, Safety, Health and Welfare Bill of 2012 (Olatubi & Olatubi, 2017).  During the above period, the Factories Act of 1987 (now known as Factories Act of 1990), which  Kalejaiye (2013) reports as a substantial revision of the Factories Act of 1958 (i.e. colonial legislation), the Workman’s Compensation Act of 1987, the Labour Act of 1990, the Workman’s Compensation Act of 2004, the Employee’s Compensation Act of 2011 (which repeals the Workman’s Compensation Act of 2004) were introduced; some of these laws are criticized as inadequate (Olatubi & Olatubi, 2017).
Also, Occupational Health and Safety Bill, 2008 was a bill to make provision for securing the safety, health and welfare of persons at work, for protecting others against risks to safety or health in connection with activities of persons at work, to establish the national council for occupational safety and health and for related issues. Okojie (2010); and Idubor & Osiamoje (2013) cited in Olatubi & Olatubi, 2017) contended that the severities of penalties stipulated by OHS laws in Nigeria are insignificant in that offenders are not deterred by the penalties. In reality there are various aspects of many of these laws that are outdated and are no longer relevance and as such not implemented   Formulation and enforcement of regulations are very vital in ensuring the efficacy of regulations. Scholars like Anderson (2007), Idubor& Osiamoje (2013) opined that formulated regulations without adequate enforcement are as good as no laws. In addition, Idubor and Osiamoje (2013) suggested that lack of strict enforcement of OSH regulations encourages non-compliance to OSH regulations with resultant increase in injuries and disease burden associated with work. Whereas non-compliance to OSH regulations is a major contributor to the poor state of OSH in Nigeria, Hezekiah et.al. (2022) maintained that the failed OSH management system in Nigeria is due to the non-functional OSH regulations and provisions.
Aside implementation challenges, many of these legislations are as old as Nigeria herself and grossly inadequate to cope with the present reality. Take for instance there are no specific regulations specifying the minimum distance of some factories or workplace that generate hazardous waste to residential buildings (Idubor & Oisamoje, 2013). It is no gain saying that as at today there is no known law in Nigeria that specifies what should be the distance of sawmill and other wood dust producing factories to residential buildings. This is also true of some other workplaces. While a lot has been documented about the utilization of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) in preventing many work related hazards among workers, only few workers used them appropriately. This might be due to non-availability of laws that spell out specific sanctions for those not complying with the use of PPE (Idubor & Oisamoje, 2013).

The genesis and evolution of Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) regulations and bills within Nigeria can be traced back to the enactment of the Labour Act in 1974, stretching through to the enactment of the Labour, Safety, Health, and Welfare Bill of 2012, as detailed by Olatubi & Olatubi (2017). Throughout this timeline, key legislative milestones include the transformation of the Factories Act from its 1987 iteration to the updated version in 1990, a significant overhaul from the colonial era Factories Act of 1958 as Kalejaiye (2013) highlights; the establishment of the Workman’s Compensation Act in both 1987 and an updated version in 2004, succeeded by the Employee’s Compensation Act of 2011 which nullified the prior version of the Workman’s Compensation Act. However, these legislative efforts have faced criticism for their perceived inadequacies (Olatubi & Olatubi, 2017).

Furthermore, the Occupational Health and Safety Bill of 2008 aimed to guarantee the safety, health, and welfare of workers, in addition to safeguarding others from workplace-related health and safety risks. It also proposed the creation of a national council for occupational safety and health among other related purposes. Yet, Okojie (2010) and Idubor & Osiamoje (2013), as referenced by Olatubi & Olatubi (2017), argue that the penalties outlined by OHS legislation in Nigeria fall short in deterring violations due to their leniency. A critical analysis reveals that several portions of these regulations have become obsolete, leading to a lack of implementation. The significance of both crafting effective regulations and ensuring their stringent enforcement has been emphasized by researchers like Anderson (2007) and Idubor & Osiamoje (2013), who argue that without rigorous enforcement, regulations are essentially null and void. Moreover, Idubor and Osiamoje (2013) attribute the lenient enforcement of OSH guidelines as a contributing factor to increased workplace injuries and illnesses.

The unsuccessful OSH management frameworks in Nigeria, as pointed out by Hezekiah et al. (2022), have been largely due to inoperative OSH regulations and provisions. Compounding the issue is the fact that many OHS legislations are exceedingly dated and do not align with the contemporary challenges, including the absence of specific guidelines around the minimum required distance of hazardous waste-producing facilities from residential areas, as highlighted by Idubor & Osiamoje (2013). Similarly, no current legislation defines the appropriate distance for sawmills and other wood dust generating factories from residential areas, showcasing gaps in regulation across various sectors. Despite extensive documentation on the importance of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) in mitigating workplace hazards, the actual usage among workers remains minimal, potentially due to the lack of enforceable laws mandating penalties for non-compliance as suggested by Idubor & Osiamoje (2013).

The Basic Conditions of Employment Act can be utilized to ensure that basic working conditions are maintained and improved (Okojie, 2010). However, it has to be acknowledged that some organisations provide more favorable benefits above the minimum and therefore, employees will always have a choice to move from one organisation to another (Olatubi, & Olatubi, 2017). The Basic Conditions of Employment Act (BCEA) if used correctly is one of the tools that are used to motivate and retain employees in various fields of work, but as previously mentioned, the issues enshrined in the BCEA are part of the keys to motivate all employees. Employees need a work environment that they like and find appropriate (Olatubi, & Olatubi, 2017). Necessary tools and proper equipment is also a prerequisite by law for all organisation as stipulated under the Occupational Health and Safety Act (2007), (G.O.K, 2007). If the workplace lacks important facilities, such as proper lighting, furniture, clean restrooms, and other health and safety provisions, employees won't be willing to put up with the inconvenience for long (Idubor & Osiamoje, 2013).

The Basic Conditions of Employment Act serves as a critical mechanism for ensuring that standard working conditions are both preserved and enhanced, as noted by Okojie (2010). It must, however, be recognized that some companies offer benefits exceeding the stipulated minimum, thus providing employees with opportunities to seek better employment packages in different organizations (Olatubi & Olatubi, 2017). When properly applied, the Basic Conditions of Employment Act (BCEA) functions as a pivotal resource for motivating and retaining staff across various employment sectors. Despite this, the provisions embedded within the BCEA represent fundamental aspects for stimulating employee motivation. Creating an employment atmosphere that is both desirable and suitable for workers is essential (Olatubi & Olatubi, 2017). Additionally, the obligation for organizations to provision necessary tools and adequate equipment as mandated by the Occupational Health and Safety Act (2007), (G.O.K, 2007), underlines the legal requirements for maintaining a conducive work environment. Should a workplace be deficient in crucial amenities like appropriate lighting, furnishings, hygienic restrooms, along with other health and safety measures, employees are likely to reject enduring such deficiencies over a prolonged period (Idubor & Osiamoje, 2013).

According to Bukunola (2018) performance appraisal is defined as a way of determining and communicating how the employees do their jobs and coming up with a plan for improving the process of carrying out work responsibilities. Performance appraisal process can also be referred to as a procedure for determining employee performance (Zehra, 2014).  Performance appraisal is essential as it gives updates on the performance of the employees; it identifies training needs and come up with plans for employee development (Usman, 2017). Performance appraisal system is usually identified as a critical element for boosting employee motivation (Okeke, 2019).  Performance appraisal system is an important drive that looks for better, more accurate, more cost for evaluating job performance and employee motivation.
According to Williams (2019), for many organisations, the primary goal of performance appraisal policy is to improve employee and organisational performance. However, a potential problem with performance appraisal policy and possible cause of much dissatisfaction, is expecting too much from one appraisal policy. Wright (2019) contended that a performance appraisal policy that is effective for developing employees may not be the best for determining pay increases. A properly designed performance appraisal policy can help achieve organisational objectives and enhance employee performance (Osmani & Maliqi, 2021).
Zehra (2014) posited that, the basic purpose of a performance appraisal policy is to improve performance of employees, teams and the entire organisation. Performance appraisal policy may also serve to assist in making administrative decisions concerning pay increases, transfers or terminations. In addition, performance appraisal policy should be legally defensible. Although a perfect performance appraisal policy does not exist, every policy should possess certain characteristics (Rahahleh, Alabaddi & Moflih, 2019). William (2019) contends that an organisation should seek an accurate investigation of performance that permits the development of a policy to improve employee performance. The policy must honestly inform employees of how they stand with the organisation.
Jokotoye  and Adigun, (2017) highlighted  that the fundamental objective of performance appraisal policy is to facilitate management in carrying out administrative decisions relating to promotions, dismissals, layoffs and pay increases. For example, the present performance of an employee is often the most significant consideration for determining whether or not to promote the employee. Managers must recognize that an employee’s development is a continuous cycle of setting performance goals, investigating performance as to the accomplishment of the goals and then setting new higher goals (Nurse, 2015).
According to Uto (2021) the individual performance of employees are compared with the standards and objectives set and agreed during the preceding appraisal meeting. Performance appraisal policy is also essential for career and succession planning. It is also of importance to the motivation of the employees, development of behaviour and attitude, alignment of the goals of individual employees and of the organisation. Moreover, it is a cultivation of a better relationship between management and employee. Formally, it serves as the written record of the performance of employees (Jokotoye, 2017).

Bukunola (2018) describes performance appraisal as a method to evaluate and communicate employees' job performance and devise strategies for enhancing the work process execution. Similarly, Zehra (2014) views it as a systematic approach to assess an employee's performance. It plays a crucial role by providing feedback on performance levels, identifying training needs, and planning for employees' development (Usman, 2017). The system of performance appraisal is deemed integral in elevating employee motivation (Okeke, 2019), serving as a substantial mechanism for assessing job performance and fostering employee motivation more effectively and economically.

Williams (2019) suggests that the primary aim of performance appraisal policies in numerous organizations is to bolster both individual and corporate performance. Nonetheless, he cautions about the potential drawback of over-reliance on a single appraisal policy, which can lead to significant dissatisfaction among staff. Wright (2019) argues that a policy adept at fostering employee growth might not be equally effective for determining salary adjustments. Osmani & Maliqi (2021) believe that a well-conceived performance appraisal policy is key to achieving business goals and improving employee performance.

The essence of performance appraisal policy, as stated by Zehra (2014), lies in its capability to enhance the performance across employees, teams, and the entire organization. Such policies not only support administrative decisions regarding remunerations, promotions, or dismissals but also should comply with legal standards. Despite the elusiveness of a faultless performance appraisal policy, it's imperative for any policy to embody specific essential traits to be effective, argue Rahahleh, Alabaddi & Moflih (2019). William (2019) advocates for an appraisal policy that accurately assesses performance, paving the way for improvement strategies and clear communication with employees regarding their position within the organization.

Jokotoye and Adigun (2017) note the primary purpose of performance appraisal policies is aiding management in making essential HR decisions such as promotions, terminations, or salary increments, often based on an employee's current performance. They emphasize the significance of recognizing employee development as an ongoing process involving goal setting, performance review, and the establishment of new, challenging objectives (Nurse, 2015).

As per Uto (2021), performance appraisals involve comparing individual employee performance against predetermined standards and objectives set during previous reviews. These appraisals are vital for career progression and succession planning, significantly impacting employee motivation, behavioral and attitudinal development, and aligning individual and organizational goals. Furthermore, they cultivate a stronger bond between management and employees and serve as formal documentation of employees' performance evaluations (Jokotoye, 2017).

One of the key responsibilities of the manager in any organisation is to evaluate the performance of his subordinates (Rahahleh, Alabaddi & Moflih, 2019). The essence of the performance appraisal is to investigate the employees’ character, attitude, potentials and past performance on the job (Mani, 2018).
According to Jolaade (2020), if the evaluation system is poor, it will not give adequate effect.  Fakeye (2018) stated that the halo effect occurs when a manager feels that the employee is particularly good in one aspect of his/her role and awards a similar high investigation for all other areas without objective consideration. Conversely, where an employee has a serious fault or poor element of performance, this can sometimes result in unjustifiably reduced investigation of other areas (Kalejaye, 2019).
Armstrong and Baron (2018) stated that each employee should be evaluated by his supervisor and then discuss set objectives for the upcoming evaluation. This discussion should cover the review of overall progress, employee development interest and needs to concentrate on specific areas of development in order to review performance objectives and performance standard.  According to Cook and Crossman (2019), performance appraisals are important for employee motivation, attitude and behaviour development, communicating organisational aims and fostering positive relationships between management and employees. Performance appraisals provide a formal, recorded, regular review of an individual's performance and a plan for future development. In short, performance appraisals are vital for managing the performance of employees in the organisation (Uto, 2021). To achieve the organisation targets there is the need for the employee and manager to discuss targets to be achieved, so that the former is genuinely able to commit to the challenge (Salami, 2020). According to Rudman (2018), performance appraisal policy was viewed as the single largest factor contributing to the development of the organisation. It gives ways to innovative thinking and a determined action to eliminate underperformance, unmotivated and poorly managed and trained employees. All of these start with making on an effective performance appraisal policy established within the organisation (Kalejaye, 2019).
Anderson (2018) highlighted that the regular and proper use of performance appraisal policy gives better results for an organisation. Performance appraisal policy may present two faces, namely the positive and negative aspects. For its positive aspect, it aids the organisation in building mechanisms that enhance performance among employees as they aim to reach the shared vision and objectives set as one body (Rudman, 2013). This is more of a discretionary effort as it increases the performance of employees and strengthens their commitment and engagement to their shared goals. For its negative aspect, performance appraisal involves a policy of appraisal which is dreaded by several managers and most of the employees such that it undermines the performance of the employees to perform better. Hence, in such case, it is considered as an irrelevant process of performance appraisal policy (Collin, 2014).

A fundamental duty of any manager within an organization involves conducting performance assessments for their team members (Rahahleh, Alabaddi & Moflih, 2019). The core intention behind performance appraisals is to delve into the employees' behavior, attitudes, capabilities, and previous job achievements (Mani, 2018).

Jolaade (2020) posits that a substandard evaluation framework yields insufficient outcomes. Fakeye (2018) points out the occurrence of the halo effect, where a manager might overly rate an employee in all areas based on excellence in a single aspect of their role without impartial judgment. On the flip side, a notable deficiency or weak performance aspect could lead to disproportionately low ratings across other competencies (Kalejaye, 2019).

According to Armstrong and Baron (2018), it is vital for every employee to undergo an assessment conducted by their direct superior, followed by discussions to set goals for the next evaluation period. These conversations should encapsulate a review of overall progress, development interests of the employee, and areas requiring focus to enhance performance standards and objectives. Cook and Crossman (2019) argue that performance evaluations play a critical role in driving employee motivation, shaping attitudes and behaviors, communicating the goals of the organization, and nurturing a constructive relationship between staff and management. Performance appraisals, therefore, constitute an essential aspect of managing employee performance within the organization by offering a structured, documented, regular assurance of an individual’s performance and a blueprint for future growth (Uto, 2021). Salami (2020) emphasizes the necessity for employees and managers to jointly decide on achievable targets, ensuring mutual commitment to these challenges. Rudman (2018) views the performance appraisal process as a pivotal contributor to organizational development, facilitating innovative thought and determined efforts to rectify issues of underperformance and inadequate management and training. Establishing an effective performance appraisal policy is the starting point for these improvements (Kalejaye, 2019).

Anderson (2018) asserts the consistent and correct application of a performance appraisal policy produces favorable outcomes for an organization. Performance appraisal practices can display both beneficial and detrimental effects. Positively, they help organizations devise strategies that improve employee performance as part of striving towards a common vision and objectives (Rudman, 2013), thereby boosting employee engagement and dedication. On the negative side, the appraisal process can be a source of dread for many managers and employees, potentially hampering the motivation to excel and thus being perceived as a superfluous element of performance management (Collin, 2014).

Yele (2019) suggested that feedback is essential for organisational effectiveness and that lack of feedback can lead to anxiety, inaccurate self-evaluations, and diversion of effort toward feedback gathering activities. Moreover, effective performance feedback has the potential to enhance employee engagement, motivation, and job satisfaction. Performance feedback is a critical component of all performance management systems. Effective performance feedback is timely, specific, behavioral in nature, and presented by a credible source.  The goals of performance feedback are to improve individual and team performance as well as employee engagement, motivation, and job satisfaction (Temidayo, 2018).
Performance feedback is effective in changing employee work behavior and it enhances employee job satisfaction and performance. The feedback should be given with a positive attitude as this can have an effect on the employees’ future performance. The result, the problems and the possible solutions are discussed with the aim of problem solving and reaching consensus (Stewart, 2013). One of the most critical parts of the appraisal process is the direct communication between supervisor and individual. In the ideal situation the employee receives information about how they are performing and where they could improve. According to Zehra (2014) managers identify the weaknesses of the employees and together they make a plan for the employee’s development.
The success of the feedback depends on the acceptance of the process. The satisfaction with the performance appraisal is an indication of the degree to which subordinates are satisfied with the process and the feedback they have received. It serves as a report of the accuracy and fair evaluations of the performance. The outcome is that satisfied individuals after the performance appraisal will improve further working relationships with supervisors and colleagues. The feedback can also bring negative reactions from employees. If perceived unfair, the feedback can cause behavioral changes such as absenteeism, lack of cooperation, lack of focus on priorities, unhealthy competition and even staff turnover. Macky and Johnson (2000) cited in Temidayo (2018) pressed that the importance of performance management system is on continuously improving organisational performance, and this is achieved by improved individual employee performance.

Yele (2019) highlighted the significance of feedback within an organization, emphasizing that its absence can lead to stress, inaccurate self-assessment, and wasted efforts in seeking feedback. Effective performance feedback, crucial to performance management systems, can significantly boost employee engagement, motivation, and satisfaction. Such feedback is most beneficial when it is timely, accurate, behavior-oriented, and delivered by a trustworthy source. The primary objectives behind providing performance feedback include enhancing the performance of both individuals and teams, alongside bolstering engagement, motivation, and overall job satisfaction among employees (Temidayo, 2018).

Feedback is pivotal in modifying employees' work behaviors and in elevating their job satisfaction and performance levels. Delivering feedback positively is crucial as it impacts the future performance of employees. Discussing outcomes, problems, and potential solutions with an aim towards problem-solving and consensus-building is essential (Stewart, 2013). Direct communication between the supervisor and the employee is a vital element of the appraisal process, where the employee learns about their current performance and areas for improvement. Zehra (2014) stated that it is through this communication that managers pinpoint areas of weakness and collaborate with employees to craft a development plan.

The effectiveness of feedback often hinges on its acceptance. The satisfaction derived from the performance appraisal process reflects the extent to which employees are content with both the process and the feedback received. This satisfaction acts as an endorsement of the evaluation's accuracy and fairness. Content individuals post-appraisal are more likely to improve their working relationships with their supervisors and colleagues. However, feedback can sometimes generate negative reactions among employees. If deemed unfair, it can lead to behavioral shifts such as absenteeism, decreased cooperation, diminished focus on primary tasks, unhealthy competitive behaviors, and even an increase in staff turnover. Macky and Johnson (2000), as cited by Temidayo (2018), argue that the essence of a performance management system lies in its capacity to continually enhance organizational performance through the improvement of individual employee performance.

Staff intentions to leave an organisation is considered to be a topic of major interest in the literature relating to organisational culture and behaviour (Yulianti, & Margaretha, 2019). For many years, turnover intention has been a critical phenomenon in managerial and administrative settings, and the issue is certainly a problem for many organisations in the modern era (Matanmi 2021). Perez (2018) defined turnover intentions as the conscious willfulness of seeking fresh job opportunities at other organisations. Meanwhile, Ugochukwu and Ndubusi (2019) have presented alternative terms for turnover intentions such as intent to leave, intention to leave, and propensity to leave. Furthermore, many researchers simply define turnover intentions as the desire or willingness to leave a position within an organisation or across organisations. 
Adeyemi (2018) posited that, researchers have tried to study the antecedents of turnover intention, in an attempt to develop managerial tools that properly deal with the problem of high rates of employee turnover.  John  (2016) stated that turnover intention is the potentiality of an individual to leave the job, which is classified into the distinction of voluntary and involuntary, as well as the functional or dysfunctional. Each type of employee turnover affects the organisation where it occurs to a varying degree. Furthermore, turnover intention is define by Hussain and Asif (2019) as the prevailing mental behavioural decisions between an employee's choices that are either to stay or withdraw, and are consequently connected instantly with actual turnover. Not surprisingly, Karatepe and Shahriari (2014) have pointed out that employees with high levels of turnover intentions have low morale, deliver poor services, and erode service recovery efforts.
Turnover intention reflects a worker's deliberate and intentional tendency to leave the job and the company. Moreover, Arshadi and Damiri (2013) defined it as the conscious decision to look for other alternative job opportunities in other organisations, and say that this results from various factors, determinants, and causes that lead employees to intend to leave. Mullins (2020) divided employee turnover intention into three particular cognitive components: thinking of leaving the job, the intention to search for another job, and then, the intention to leave. Benson (2019) argued that there is a connection between actual employee turnover and turnover intention. Thus, this intention to leave creates direct effects on turnover decisions. An employee’s decision to leave has several unwanted consequences, both for the organisation and for the employee, which functions along many dimensions. 
Based on the research by Nyamubarwa (2013), the intention to leave from an organisation is perceived to be part of a set of consecutive steps in the psychological withdrawal of an employee from the job process. Scholars and researchers have claimed that employers focus minimal attention to this serious behaviour, yet employers have to deal with the actual turnover that is a logical consequence of the intention to leave. Additionally, reasons for the actual turnover of employees might still be somewhat vague for employers, as they are not able to gain access to employees who have already left the company in order to ascertain what went wrong. Thus, studies that discuss intention to leave are potentially more useful and applicable, than those which address actual turnover behaviour (Rehman, 2012). 
Employees who are thinking of leaving might still be persuaded to stay within the organisation if employers knew what they were dealing with. “Intention to leave”, then, refers to the subjective estimation of an individual's probability of leaving an organisation in the near future (Nyamubarwa, 2013). Additionally, Remilekun (2014) describes intention to leave as the antecedent to actual turnover behaviour in many turnover models. In other words, it is appeared that few theorists have noted that it is, in fact, turnover intention which is the most powerful and interesting predictor of actual turnover.

Yulianti and Margaretha (2019) have identified the subject of staff intentions to leave an organization as a significant area of focus in studies related to organizational culture and behavior. The phenomenon of turnover intention has long been a pivotal concern within managerial and administrative contexts, posing challenges for numerous organizations today (Matanmi 2021). Perez (2018) characterizes turnover intentions as the deliberate pursuit of new employment opportunities in different organizations. Similarly, Ugochukwu and Ndubusi (2019) refer to turnover intentions with variations like intent to leave, intention to leave, and propensity to leave, aligning with the broader understanding of it as the desire or determination to exit one's current role either within the same organization or to another.

Researchers, as noted by Adeyemi (2018), have delved into exploring the precursors to turnover intention with the aim of crafting management strategies to mitigate the high rates of employee turnover effectively. John (2016) categorizes turnover intention into voluntary vs. involuntary and functional vs. dysfunctional, indicating the varied impact each type has on the organization affected. Hussain and Asif (2019) further expand on this by defining turnover intention as the ongoing mental and behavioral decision-making process where an employee contemplates staying or departing, which closely links to the actual turnover behavior. This connection between high turnover intentions and negative outcomes such as low morale, subpar service delivery, and compromised service recovery efforts was highlighted by Karatepe and Shahriari (2014).

Arshadi and Damiri (2013) describe turnover intention as the conscious inclination towards seeking alternative employment, driven by various factors and causes. Mullins (2020) breaks down employee turnover intention into three cognitive stages: contemplating leaving, intending to search for another job, and finally, deciding to leave. Benson (2019) establishes a link between employees' turnover intention and their eventual departure, emphasizing the direct influence that intention has on turnover decisions. The consequences of an employee's decision to leave are manifold and undesirable, affecting both the organization and the employee across various dimensions.

Nyamubarwa (2013) views the intention to leave as a part of a series of steps in an employee's psychological withdrawal from their job, a critical perspective often overlooked by employers who are then faced with the tangible outcome of actual turnover. The challenge for employers lies in the difficulty of understanding the reasons behind employee departures, especially when direct communication with those who have left is out of reach, making studies on turnover intentions particularly valuable and relevant (Rehman, 2012).

Understanding what prompts employees to consider leaving is crucial, as it opens up the possibility for organizations to intervene and possibly retain these individuals. The term "intention to leave" signifies an individual's subjective likelihood of exiting an organization in the foreseeable future (Nyamubarwa, 2013). Moreover, Remilekun (2014) positions intention to leave as a precursor to actual turnover in various models, illustrating the consensus among some theorists that turnover intention is indeed a critical and insightful predictor of actual turnover.

Singh (2014) reported that one of the biggest challenges currently faced by numerous organisations is high labour turnover. This has proven to be problematic due to the costs that organisations encounter. According to Mengel (2017), about 80% of labour turnover is as a result of poor hiring and selection, thus inadequate talent management practices. Harris and Williamson (2018) state that new employees and long-term employees usually have different reasons for leaving the organisation. With new employees, turnover is usually as a result of poor induction and orientation, counter-offers, mismatch between the experience at the workplace and the expectations of the employee (Joubert, 2013). Hunt (2014) argued that the staffing department should actively work with other human resources department such as compensation, performance management and employee development to ensure that the employer brand being promised to potential candidates during the recruiting process is fulfilled after the individuals are hired. Mustapha (2019) adds that, on the other hand, employees leave their organisations for different reasons which include: 
i.A mismatch between performance and pay. 
ii.A lack of growth and advancement opportunities for employees. 
iii.Unrealistic and unclear expectations from management. 
iv.The job being regarded as unimportant. 
v.Individuals feeling that their abilities are underutilized. 
vi.Individuals feel unappreciated. 
vii.A feeling that there is no support from management and limited resources are allocated. 
Gboyega (2017) concurs with Harris and Williamson (2018) that there are various factors which cause people to leave an organisation which include; a lack of control over one’s work, job insecurity, better working conditions and compensations offered elsewhere, repetitive tasks and little to no new challenges, poor organisational performance raising questions about its future.

Singh (2014) identified one of the most pressing issues organizations face today as high employee turnover, which carries significant financial costs for the companies involved. Mengel (2017) attributed roughly 80% of this turnover to deficient hiring and selection processes, pointing to subpar talent management practices as the root cause. Harris and Williamson (2018) noted that the motivations for leaving an organization tend to differ between newer and more established employees. For those recently hired, turnover often stems from inadequate induction and orientation programs, competitive counter-offers, and a disparity between the employee's expectations and the reality of the workplace environment (Joubert, 2013). Hunt (2014) emphasized the importance of recruitment teams collaborating with other HR sectors, such as compensation, performance management, and employee development, to ensure that the employer's brand commitments made during recruitment are actualized post-hire.

Mustapha (2019) expanded on the various reasons employees might choose to leave an organization, which can include:
i. A disconnect between performance and remuneration.
ii. Limited opportunities for growth and progression within the company.
iii. Ambiguous and unrealistic expectations set by management.
iv. The perception of one's role as insignificant.
v. A sense of underutilization concerning one's skills.
vi. A general feeling of being underappreciated.
vii. The absence of managerial support and insufficient resources.

Gboyega (2017) agreed with Harris and Williamson (2018), highlighting additional factors that drive individuals to resign, such as a lack of autonomy in their work, job insecurity, more attractive employment terms elsewhere, monotonous tasks without the opportunity for new challenges, and poor company performance that triggers concerns about its viability and future direction.

Employees leave organisation based on different reasons and each reason will have its deferent consequences for the organisation so to make them more clear Aljunaibi  (2014)  pointed out that there are two types of turnover; voluntary and involuntary; a voluntary turnover decision is made by the employee it means when an employee decides to leave organisation willingly but the involuntary turnover decision is made by the employer and it happens when an employer fire or dismiss an employee (Aljunaibi, 2014).
Furthermore, the author explains that voluntary turnover can be either avoidable or unavoidable; avoidable turnover is the one which can be controlled or influenced by the organisation as an example when employee try to leave because of low job satisfaction so the organisation will improve the job situation by giving more chance to the employee to develop his/her skills but from another side unavoidable turnover is the one which cannot be controlled or influenced by the organisation for instance if an employee gets sick and cannot continue the job, what the author suggests is that the organisation must be cautious about unavoidable turnover because it can cost a lot to the organisation (Huselid, 1995.) .

Aljunaibi (2014) distinguished between two primary categories of employee turnover: voluntary and involuntary. Voluntary turnover occurs when an employee opts to leave an organization of their own accord, whereas involuntary turnover transpires when the decision to terminate an employee’s position comes from the employer, resulting in the employee being fired or laid off.

Additionally, the author elaborates that within voluntary turnover, there exist subcategories of avoidable and unavoidable turnover. Avoidable turnover refers to situations where the organization has the potential to retain the employee by addressing and rectifying the underlying issues causing dissatisfaction. For example, if an employee contemplates leaving due to low job satisfaction, the organization can intervene by offering opportunities for skill development, thereby improving the job experience for the employee. On the contrary, unavoidable turnover includes scenarios beyond the organization's control, such as an employee having to resign due to health issues. Aljunaibi highlights the importance for organizations to be particularly vigilant regarding unavoidable turnover, as it can pose significant costs to the organization, underscored by Huselid in 1995, indicating the financial and operational impacts of turnover that cannot be influenced by the organization's interventions or policies.

a. Functional vs. Dysfunctional Turnover: 
Functional turnover can be defined as "A turnover in which poor performers leave" while Dysfunctional turnover can be defined as "A turnover in which good performers leave" 

b. Avoidable vs. Unavoidable Turnover: 
A turnover that happens in avoidable circumstances is called 'Avoidable Turnover' whereas a turnover that happens in unavoidable circumstances is called 'Unavoidable Turnover' (Ibid). 
c. Voluntary v/s Involuntary Turnover: 
 Akintayo, (2007) defined voluntary turnover as individual movement across membership boundary of a social system which is initiated by the individual, where involuntary turnover can be defined as "The turnover in which employees have no choice in their termination e.g. sickness, death, moving abroad or employer's initiated termination" 
d. Internal vs. External Turnover: 
Internal turnover happens when employees send-off their current position and getting a new position within the same organisation (Babajide, 2010). It is related with the internal recruitment where organisations filling the vacant position by their employee or recruiting within the organisation (Babajide, 2010). External turnover arises when an employee separates the organisation to join another. This may happen due to voluntary or involuntary reasons. 
e. Skilled vs. Unskilled Turnover: 
Untrained, uneducated and unskilled positions often face high turnover rate. Without the organisation or business incurring any loss of performance, employees can generally be replaced (Adi, 2016). On the other hand skilled and educated positions may create a risk to the organisation while leaving ((Adi, 2016). Therefore turnover for skilled and educated professionals incur replacement costs as well as competitive disadvantage of the business.

a. **Functional vs. Dysfunctional Turnover:** 
Functional turnover refers to the departure of employees who are underperforming or are a poor fit for the organization, potentially benefiting the organization by their leaving. Conversely, dysfunctional turnover involves the loss of proficient and high-performing employees, which can adversely impact the organization.

b. **Avoidable vs. Unavoidable Turnover:** 
Turnover categorized as 'Avoidable' occurs under circumstances where the organization could have taken actions to retain the employee, addressing the underlying issues leading to the turnover. 'Unavoidable Turnover' refers to cases where the employee's departure is due to reasons beyond the organization's control or influence, such as personal health issues or relocation.

c. **Voluntary vs. Involuntary Turnover:** 
Defined by Akintayo (2007), voluntary turnover is when an employee decides to leave an organization on their own accord, crossing the boundary of membership from being an insider to an outsider of the social system. In contrast, involuntary turnover happens when employees are forced to leave due to reasons beyond their control, such as health problems, death, moving to other countries, or termination initiated by the employer.

d. **Internal vs. External Turnover:** 
Internal turnover occurs when employees transition from their current roles to new positions within the same organization, as mentioned by Babajide (2010). It is often associated with internal recruitment strategies, where vacancies are filled by promoting or transferring existing employees. External turnover, on the other hand, happens when employees leave the organization to take positions in different organizations, for either voluntary or involuntary reasons.

e. **Skilled vs. Unskilled Turnover:** 
Positions that require minimal training, education, or skilled expertise tend to experience high turnover rates, with little to no detriment to the organization's overall performance, as employees in these roles can be more easily replaced (Adi, 2016). In contrast, turnover among highly skilled and educated employees poses a greater risk to the organization, as their departure not only incurs replacement costs but can also lead to a competitive disadvantage, given the specialized knowledge and expertise they take with them (Adi, 2016).

Employee turnover is expensive from the view of the organisation. Hence, it results subsequent replacement process entails manifold costs to the organisations. These replacement costs include for example, search of the external labor market for a possible substitute, selection between competing substitutes, induction of the chosen substitute, and formal and informal training of the substitute until he or she attains performance levels equivalent to the individual who quit (John 2000). Many researchers argue that high turnover rates might have negative effects on the profitability of organisations if not managed properly (Hogan 1992); (Wasmuth & Davis, 1993) and (Barrows 1990). Turnover has many hidden or invisible costs Philips (1990) and these invisible costs are result of incoming employees, co-workers closely associated with incoming employees, co-workers closely associated with departing employees and position being filled while vacant. And all these affect the profitability of the organisation. On the other hand turnover affects customer service and satisfaction (Adeboye & Adegoroye 2012). Catherine (2002) argued that turnover include other costs, such as lost productivity; loss of sales, and management's time. This clearly demonstrates that turnover affects the profitability of the organisation and if it's not managed properly it would have a negative effect on the profit. Each time an employee leaves the firm, we presume that productivity drops due to the learning curve involved in understanding the job and the organisation. Furthermore, the loss of intellectual capital adds to this cost, since not only do organisations lose the human capital and relational capital of the departing employee, but also competitors are potentially gaining these assets (Meaghan et al. 2002). Therefore, if employee turnover is not managed properly it would affect the organisation adversely in terms of personnel costs and in the long run it would affect its liquidity position.

Employee turnover represents a significant financial strain for organizations, primarily due to the multifaceted costs associated with replacing departing employees. These expenses entail scouting the external labor market for potential candidates, evaluating and selecting among them, onboarding the chosen individual, and providing both formal and informal training until the new hire reaches the productivity level of the predecessor (John 2000). Numerous scholars have indicated that unchecked high turnover rates could detrimentally affect organizational profitability (Hogan 1992; Wasmuth & Davis, 1993; Barrows 1990). Philips (1990) highlighted the often overlooked or "invisible" costs of turnover, which emerge not only from integrating new employees but also from the impact on coworkers of both the incoming and departing staff, as well as the operational losses incurred during periods when positions remain unfilled. These aspects collectively undermine the organization’s profitability.

Moreover, turnover has been identified as a factor that negatively influences customer service and satisfaction (Adeboye & Adegoroye 2012). Catherine (2002) further elaborated on the costs associated with turnover, including diminished productivity, loss of sales, and the diversion of managerial time and efforts. This underscores the broader implications of turnover on the profitability of an organization and highlights the risk that, if not effectively managed, turnover could substantially erode profit margins. The departure of an employee often leads to an immediate decrease in productivity due to the time required for new hires to acclimate to their roles and the organizational environment. Additionally, the exit of employees translates to a loss of intellectual capital, adversely affecting the organization not only through the loss of human and relational capital but also by potentially enhancing the competitive position of rivals should they hire the departing employees (Meaghan et al. 2002). Consequently, ineffective management of employee turnover can negatively impact an organization by increasing personnel costs and, over time, adversely affecting its liquidity position.

The cost associated with  employee turnover  is enormous,  Ashraf,  Caldwell (2017) enumerated the costs of turnover to include, recruiting costs, selection and or employment costs, orientation costs, training costs, lost wages/salaries, administrative costs, lost productivity, loss of human capital, and customer satisfaction issues. The common way of investigating employee resignation is by conducting an exit interview which also is another cost to the Organisation. In conducting an exit interview, some costs are involved and they must be taken into consideration when calculating turnover costs. Bliss (2017) listed these costs to include the time of the person conducting the interview and the administrative costs involved in processing the resignation letter, including stationery and printing.

The financial repercussions of employee turnover are substantial, with Ashraf and Caldwell (2017) detailing a broad array of incurred expenses. These encompass recruiting and hiring costs, orientation expenses, investment in training and development, alongside the direct financial loss from unfilled work hours or salaries. Additionally, administrative costs, a decline in productivity, erosion of human capital, and adverse impacts on customer satisfaction further amplify the financial strain on an organization. A commonly adopted methodology for exploring the reasons behind employee departures is the exit interview, which itself introduces additional costs to the organization. Bliss (2017) itemized these exit interview expenses, highlighting the time investment required from the interviewer and the administrative charges related to processing the resignation, which includes stationery and printing costs. These facets are crucial considerations in the overall assessment of the financial impact employee turnover has on an organization.

Performance is an important tool used to determine the accomplishment of a particular task in an organisation (Faruna, 2018). Performance refers to how well a task assigned to a person is been done.  Cook & Crossman (2019) defined performance as the achievement of specific tasks measured against predetermined or identified standards of accuracy, completeness, cost and speed. While performance may be used to define what an organisation has accomplished with respect to the process, results, relevance and success (Debowale, 2021). Employee performance on the other hand is defined as the outcome or contribution of employees to make them attain goals (Jolaade 2018). Employee performance can be manifested in improvement in production, easiness in using the new technology, highly motivated workers. Sheehan (2014) viewed employee performance as the overall task execution carried out by employees. The author stressed that proper investigation of work performance must include grouping, assigning, analyzing human resource expertise as well as managerial personnel to carry out responsibility due to their knowledge of total production and workflow. 
Hunt (2014) stated that to define employee performance, organisations use different measures which include; the achievement of set goals, that is, whether or not employees accomplish the objectives which are assigned to them. Employee performance is also defined by the demonstration of competencies determined by whether employees behave in a way that is expected of them on their jobs (Siddique, 2004). Employees must know what they need to do to perform their job successfully. Setting performance expectations and goals for individuals and groups to channel their efforts towards achieving organisational objectives. Getting employees involved in the planning process helps them understand the goals of the organisation, what needs to be done, why it needs to be done and how well it should be done (Frye, 2014). Performance expectations need to be understood and where possible, to involve the contribution from the employees as Voigreddy (2020) puts it. Guest (2017) argued that as individuals cannot always control their results, it‘s important to have behavioral targets as well as output targets. It is recommended that there is a personal development plan which would again underpin the achievement of objectives. Bolarinwa (2015) stated that managing employee performance every day is the key to an effective performance management system. Setting goals, making sure expectations are clear and providing frequent feedback help people perform most effectively. 
Goal setting involves managers and subordinates jointly establishing and clarifying employees ‘goals. It affects performance through influencing what people think and do by focusing their behavior in the direction of goals, energize behavior, motivate people to put forth the effort to reach difficult goals that are expected and clarifies duties and responsibilities. Participation convinces employees that the goals are achievable and can increase motivation and performance (Snell, 2017). Clear performance expectations are a critical factor in teamwork success, whether your goal is to develop a project team, your departmental team, or a sense of teamwork company-wide, clear performance expectations support teamwork success. Use clear performance expectations to help employees develop accountable, productive, meaningful, participatory teamwork.  Crossman (2019) highlighted that achieving goals is a function of the competencies people demonstrate on their jobs which depends on the skills and other attributes.

Performance is a critical measure utilized to assess the completion of specific tasks within an organization, according to Faruna (2018). It gauges the extent to which an assigned activity is successfully executed. Cook & Crossman (2019) articulate performance as the attainment of particular tasks gauged against pre-established standards covering accuracy, completeness, cost, and speed. Performance also encapsulates the broader achievements of an organization, including process efficiency, results, relevance, and overall success (Debowale, 2021). On the individual level, employee performance is the contributions or outcomes employees deliver towards achieving organizational goals (Jolaade 2018), which may manifest as enhanced production, adeptness in adopting new technology, and a workforce that is highly motivated. Sheehan (2014) proposes that a thorough analysis of work performance encompasses the organization, delegation, and evaluation of human resource capabilities, including management, to fulfill responsibilities aligned with the knowledge of overall production and workflow processes.

To delineate employee performance, organizations employ various metrics such as the attainment of predefined objectives and the demonstration of required competencies, which assess if employees are acting in ways that meet job expectations (Siddique, 2004). It is essential for employees to understand the prerequisites for executing their roles effectively. Setting clear performance objectives for both individuals and teams directs their efforts towards the realization of organizational ambitions. Engaging employees in the planning phase enables them to grasp the organizational goals, the necessary actions to achieve these goals, the rationale behind these actions, and the expected standards of execution (Frye, 2014). According to Voigreddy (2020), performance expectations should be comprehensible to employees, involving their input wherever feasible. Guest (2017) emphasizes the importance of setting behavioral targets alongside output targets due to the variability in individuals' control over results, advocating for personal development plans to support objective attainment.

Bolarinwa (2015) asserts that daily management of employee performance is vital to an effective performance management framework, highlighting the significance of goal setting, clarity in expectations, and regular feedback to optimize employee output. Goal setting, as described by Snell (2017), involves collaborative efforts between management and staff to specify and clarify work goals, thereby focusing and energizing behavior towards these goals, motivating employees to strive for challenging objectives, and elucidating roles and responsibilities. Participation in goal setting can enhance employees' belief in the attainability of these goals, thereby fostering motivation and improving performance. Clear performance expectations are crucial for successful teamwork across various organizational levels, facilitating the development of accountable, productive, and engaged teams (Crossman, 2019), with achievement being predicated on the competencies demonstrated in the workplace, contingent upon skills and other personal attributes.

Quality is a necessity for all companies’ services, industrial, general or private as it forms a principal factor of customers’ satisfaction.
h-Company's Reputation: The Company gets fame and reputation through the quality of its products and services (Adamu, 2018).. This is shown through the relations that link the company to the suppliers in addition to the workers' experience and the trial of presenting products satisfying the needs and desires of clients (Khan & Jabbar, 2013). In case that these products were with low quality, the quality should be promoted to achieve the reputation that enables the company competing the similar companies in the same field (Hassan, S. (2018).
i- Flexibility: Flexibility is the company’s ability to provide a variety and different levels in the target market through its ability to keep up with the developments in technology and to provide products and services according to customer expectations (Hassan, 2018). The ability of the companies respond to the changes in the customer needs and wants quickly and efficiently. The low cost and the best quality are no more regarded as a competitive distinction after the increase of the change and variety of the clients' desires; however, the price and quality are two core issues in the products of many business companies. As a result, the flexibility has become the crucial competitive dimension represented in the ability of the company in adaption for providing specific products and services.
j-Innovation: Innovations are new ideas that consist of new products and services, new use of existing products and services or new markets for existing products or new marketing methods. It is also known as a process that provides added value and a degree of novelty to the company, suppliers and customers, developing new procedures, solutions, products and services and new ways of marketing. The ability of a company to put forward an idea of a higher value can be a source of competitive advantage and represents the reason for which a customer may opt for one company to the disadvantage of another (Urbancova, 2013).

Quality stands as a critical component for the operations of all companies, whether they are in the service, industrial, or private sectors, acting as a key driver of customer satisfaction.

h- **Company's Reputation:** A company secures its standing and reputation in the market through the quality of the products and services it offers (Adamu, 2018). The strength of a company's reputation is often reflected in its relationships with suppliers, the expertise of its workforce, and its capacity to meet and exceed the needs and desires of its clients (Khan & Jabbar, 2013). Should the quality of these products fall short, efforts must be made to enhance quality to attain a competitive level of reputation that allows the company to effectively compete with peers in the same sector (Hassan, S. (2018).

i- **Flexibility:** Flexibility refers to a company's capability to offer a diverse range of products and services tailored to meet the evolving technological advancements and customer expectations (Hassan, 2018). It involves the ability to swiftly and efficiently adapt to changing consumer demands. In a landscape where low cost and superior quality no longer serve as unique competitive edges due to the growing variation in consumer desires, both price and quality remain fundamental in many corporate offerings. Consequently, flexibility emerges as a pivotal competitive aspect, denoted by a company's adeptness at adapting and providing particular products and services tailored to specific demands.

j-**Innovation:** Innovation encompasses the introduction of new ideas, which may include launching new products and services, finding new applications for existing offerings, expanding into new markets, or employing novel marketing strategies. It is characterized as a process that adds value and introduces a novel aspect to the company, its suppliers, and customers by developing fresh procedures, solutions, products, services, and marketing approaches. The capacity of a company to generate ideas of higher value can serve as a significant competitive edge and drive customer preference towards it over competitors (Urbancova, 2013).

Various theories underpin the understanding of the influence of integrated management strategies on employees’ turnover intentions and job performance. Prominent among these theories and of particular relevance to the present study are: Job Embededness theory, Resource Based View (RBV) Theory, Intellectual Capital Theory, Employee Recognition Theory, Vrooms’ Expectancy Theory and Adams’s Equity Theory.
2.2.1 Job Embeddedness Theory
In 2001, Mitchell et aI. (2001) introduced a new conceptual framework called job embeddedness. Derived from Lewin's field theory, job embeddedness "represents a broad constellation of influences" (Mitchell et aI. 2001, p. 7) on an employee's intent to stay and can be thought of as "a net or a web in which one can become stuck" (Mitchell et aI., 2001, p. 7).
Mitchell et al. (2001) clarified that "job embeddedness does not cause one to go out and get married, buy a house, or increase linkages with the organisation," but "those activities cause a person to become embedded" (p. 25). They further proposed that job embeddedness represented a unique factor in understanding why people stay at their jobs and discussed ways in which employers can influence employees' embeddedness and propensity to stay. They recommended examining three components-links, fit, and sacrifice-each in an organisational and community context. It is this examination of both the organisation and community that makes job embeddedness unique in helping explain employee retention.
In Mitchell et aI. (2001) original job embeddedness study, the authors used chi square and correlational statistics and found that job embeddedness improved the prediction of turnover over and above that provided by job satisfaction, organisation commitment, and intent to stay (Mitchell et aI., 2001, p. 30). The authors concluded that the job-satisfaction and organisation-commitment frameworks insufficiently explain why people stay in an organisation, because they ignore the effects of an employee's nonwork life. 
2.2.2 Resource Based View Theory 
The Resource Based View (RBV) was put forward by Wernerfelt in 1984. This theory holds the proposition that, the most important determinant of a firm’s performance is the resources that the firm possesses which are more of a competitive advantage to the firm (Wernerfelt, 2007 cited   Kolawole, 2021). According to Ling and Jaw (2019), the firms’ resources can be categorized in several ways from all the assets, the processes and even the knowledge that is posed by the firm that facilitates effective and efficient performance. The RBV theory of the firm provided that the firm gets added value through identification of its unique resources then putting strategies in place to ensure  that those resources are well developed in a rare and inimitable way such that they cannot be substituted by the competitors (Wright,2019). Employee’s talents are resources within an organisation (Maina, 2019).  Thus talent development involves the attraction of the most valuable unique talent and ensuring that the talented employee has been given an added value in achievement of both the current and future expectations of the organisation.

The understanding of how integrated management strategies impact employee turnover intentions and job performance is enhanced by several theories. Notably, Job Embeddedness theory, Resource Based View (RBV) Theory, Intellectual Capital Theory, Employee Recognition Theory, Vroom's Expectancy Theory, and Adams’s Equity Theory offer valuable insights.

**2.2.1 Job Embeddedness Theory**

Introduced by Mitchell et al. in 2001, the concept of job embeddedness offers a comprehensive understanding of the factors influencing an employee's intention to remain with an employer. Rooted in Lewin's field theory, it is depicted as a complex network of influences that essentially 'entangle' an employee, making it less likely for them to leave. Mitchell et al. highlighted that personal choices such as marrying or buying a house, rather than directly influencing job embeddedness, contribute to one becoming more embedded through enhancing links to the organization and community. This theory emphasizes examining three key components - links, fit, and sacrifice - within both organizational and community contexts to fully understand employee retention. Their foundational study revealed that job embeddedness more accurately predicts turnover intentions than traditional measures such as job satisfaction and organizational commitment, underscoring the importance of considering an employee's life outside work.

**2.2.2 Resource Based View Theory**

The Resource Based View (RBV) theory, advanced by Wernerfelt in 1984, posits that a firm’s resources, which provide a competitive advantage, are pivotal in determining its performance. These resources can range from tangible assets to processes and knowledge that enable the firm to operate effectively and efficiently. The core premise of the RBV theory is that firms create added value and sustain competitive advantage by identifying unique resources and developing them in ways that are rare, valuable, and difficult for competitors to imitate or substitute. According to Ling and Jaw (2019), this encompasses all aspects of a firm's operations, including the talent within an organization. Maina (2019) asserts that employee talents are indeed resources that must be attracted, nurtured, and developed to meet and exceed the current and future goals of the organization, thereby achieving a strategic advantage.
